Executes a given bash command in a persistent shell session with optional timeout, ensuring proper handling and security measures.

Be aware: OS: linux, Shell: bash

All commands run in the current working directory by default. Use the `workdir` parameter if you need to run a command in a different directory. AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>` patterns - use `workdir` instead.

Use `/tmp/opencode` for temporary work outside the workspace. This directory has already been created, already exists, and is pre-approved for external directory access.

IMPORTANT: This tool is for terminal operations like git, npm, docker, etc. DO NOT use it for file operations (reading, writing, editing, searching, finding files) - use the specialized tools for this instead.

Before executing the command, please follow these steps:

1. Directory Verification:
   - If the command will create new directories or files, first use `ls` to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location
   - For example, before running "mkdir foo/bar", first use `ls foo` to check that "foo" exists and is the intended parent directory

2. Command Execution:
   - Always quote file paths that contain spaces with double quotes (e.g., rm "path with spaces/file.txt")
   - Examples of proper quoting:
     - mkdir "/Users/name/My Documents" (correct)
     - mkdir /Users/name/My Documents (incorrect - will fail)
     - python "/path/with spaces/script.py" (correct)
     - python /path/with spaces/script.py (incorrect - will fail)
   - After ensuring proper quoting, execute the command.
   - Capture the output of the command.

Usage notes:
  - The command argument is required.
  - You can specify an optional timeout in milliseconds. If not specified, commands will time out after 120000ms (2 minutes).
  - It is very helpful if you write a clear, concise description of what this command does in 5-10 words.
  - If the output exceeds 2000 lines or 51200 bytes, it will be truncated and the full output will be written to a file. You can use Read with offset/limit to read specific sections or Grep to search the full content. Do NOT use `head`, `tail`, or other truncation commands to limit output; the full output will already be captured to a file for more precise searching.

  - Avoid using Bash with the `find`, `grep`, `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `sed`, `awk`, or `echo` commands, unless explicitly instructed or when these commands are truly necessary for the task. Instead, always prefer using the dedicated tools for these commands:
    - File search: Use Glob (NOT find or ls)
    - Content search: Use Grep (NOT grep or rg)
    - Read files: Use Read (NOT cat/head/tail)
    - Edit files: Use Edit (NOT sed/awk)
    - Write files: Use Write (NOT echo >/cat <<EOF)
    - Communication: Output text directly (NOT echo/printf)
  - When issuing multiple commands:
    - If the commands are independent and can run in parallel, make multiple Bash tool calls in a single message. For example, if you need to run "git status" and "git diff", send a single message with two Bash tool calls in parallel.
    - If the commands depend on each other and must run sequentially, use a single Bash call with '&&' to chain them together (e.g., `git add . && git commit -m "message" && git push`). For instance, if one operation must complete before another starts (like mkdir before cp, Write before Bash for git operations, or git add before git commit), run these operations sequentially instead.
    - Use ';' only when you need to run commands sequentially but don't care if earlier commands fail
    - DO NOT use newlines to separate commands (newlines are ok in quoted strings)
  - AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>`. Use the `workdir` parameter to change directories instead.
    <good-example>
    Use workdir="/foo/bar" with command: pytest tests
    </good-example>
    <bad-example>
    cd /foo/bar && pytest tests
    </bad-example>

# Committing changes with git

Only create commits when requested by the user. If unclear, ask first. When the user asks you to create a new git commit, follow these steps carefully:

Git Safety Protocol:
- NEVER update the git config
- NEVER run destructive/irreversible git commands (like push --force, hard reset, etc) unless the user explicitly requests them
- NEVER skip hooks (--no-verify, --no-gpg-sign, etc) unless the user explicitly requests it
- NEVER run force push to main/master, warn the user if they request it
- Avoid git commit --amend. ONLY use --amend when ALL conditions are met:
  (1) User explicitly requested amend, OR the commit succeeded and pre-commit hooks auto-modified files that need including — verify by checking `git log` that HEAD is the new commit before amending
  (2) HEAD commit was created by you in this conversation (verify: git log -1 --format='%an %ae')
  (3) Commit has NOT been pushed to remote (verify: git status shows "Your branch is ahead")
- CRITICAL: If commit FAILED or was REJECTED by hook, NEVER amend - fix the issue and create a NEW commit
- CRITICAL: If you already pushed to remote, NEVER amend unless user explicitly requests it (requires force push)
- NEVER commit changes unless the user explicitly asks you to. It is VERY IMPORTANT to only commit when explicitly asked, otherwise the user will feel that you are being too proactive.

1. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following bash commands in parallel, each using the Bash tool:
  - Run a git status command to see all untracked files.
  - Run a git diff command to see both staged and unstaged changes that will be committed.
  - Run a git log command to see recent commit messages, so that you can follow this repository's commit message style.
2. Analyze all staged changes (both previously staged and newly added) and draft a commit message:
  - Summarize the nature of the changes (eg. new feature, enhancement to an existing feature, bug fix, refactoring, test, docs, etc.). Ensure the message accurately reflects the changes and their purpose (i.e. "add" means a wholly new feature, "update" means an enhancement to an existing feature, "fix" means a bug fix, etc.).
  - Do not commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc.). Warn the user if they specifically request to commit those files
  - Draft a concise (1-2 sentences) commit message that focuses on the "why" rather than the "what"
  - Ensure it accurately reflects the changes and their purpose
3. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following commands:
   - Add relevant untracked files to the staging area.
   - Create the commit with a message
   - Run git status after the commit completes to verify success.
   Note: git status depends on the commit completing, so run it sequentially after the commit.
4. If the commit fails due to pre-commit hook, fix the issue and create a NEW commit (see amend rules above)

Important notes:
- NEVER run additional commands to read or explore code, besides git bash commands
- NEVER use the TodoWrite or Task tools
- DO NOT push to the remote repository unless the user explicitly asks you to do so
- IMPORTANT: Never use git commands with the -i flag (like git rebase -i or git add -i) since they require interactive input which is not supported.
- If there are no changes to commit (i.e., no untracked files and no modifications), do not create an empty commit

When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following bash commands in parallel using the Bash tool, in order to understand the current state of the branch since it diverged from the main branch:

2. Analyze all changes that will be included in the pull request, making sure to look at all relevant commits (NOT just the latest commit, but ALL commits that will be included in the pull request!!!), and draft a pull request summary
3. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following commands in parallel:

Use this tool to create and manage a structured task list for your current coding session. This helps you track progress, organize complex tasks, and demonstrate thoroughness to the user.
It also helps the user understand the progress of the task and overall progress of their requests.

## When to Use This Tool
Use this tool proactively in these scenarios:

1. Complex multistep tasks - When a task requires 3 or more distinct steps or actions
2. Non-trivial and complex tasks - Tasks that require careful planning or multiple operations
3. User explicitly requests todo list - When the user directly asks you to use the todo list
4. User provides multiple tasks - When users provide a list of things to be done (numbered or comma-separated)
5. After receiving new instructions - Immediately capture user requirements as todos. Feel free to edit the todo list based on new information.
6. After completing a task - Mark it complete and add any new follow-up tasks
7. When you start working on a new task, mark the todo as in_progress. Ideally you should only have one todo as in_progress at a time. Complete existing tasks before starting new ones.

## When NOT to Use This Tool

Skip using this tool when:
1. There is only a single, straightforward task
2. The task is trivial and tracking it provides no organizational benefit
3. The task can be completed in less than 3 trivial steps
4. The task is purely conversational or informational

NOTE that you should not use this tool if there is only one trivial task to do. In this case you are better off just doing the task directly.

## Examples of When to Use the Todo List

<example>
User: I want to add a dark mode toggle to the application settings. Make sure you run the tests and build when you're done!
Assistant: I'll help add a dark mode toggle to your application settings. Let me create a todo list to track this implementation.
*Creates todo list with the following items:*
1. Create dark mode toggle component in Settings page
2. Add dark mode state management (context/store)
3. Implement CSS-in-JS styles for dark theme
4. Update existing components to support theme switching
5. Run tests and build process, addressing any failures or errors that occur
*Begins working on the first task*

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. Adding dark mode is a multi-step feature requiring UI, state management, and styling changes
2. The user explicitly requested tests and build be run afterward
3. The assistant inferred that tests and build need to pass by adding "Ensure tests and build succeed" as the final task
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Help me rename the function getCwd to getCurrentWorkingDirectory across my project
Assistant: Let me first search through your codebase to find all occurrences of 'getCwd'.
*Uses grep or search tools to locate all instances of getCwd in the codebase*
Assistant: I've found 15 instances of 'getCwd' across 8 different files. Let me create a todo list to track these changes.
*Creates todo list with specific items for each file that needs updating*

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. First, the assistant searched to understand the scope of the task
2. Upon finding multiple occurrences across different files, it determined this was a complex task with multiple steps
3. The todo list helps ensure every instance is tracked and updated systematically
4. This approach prevents missing any occurrences and maintains code consistency
</reasoning>
</example>


<example>
User: I need to implement these features for my e-commerce site: user registration, product catalog, shopping cart, and checkout flow.
Assistant: I'll help implement these features. First, let's add all the features to the todo list.
*Creates a todo list breaking down each feature into specific tasks based on the project architecture*
Assistant: Let's start with user registration. This will involve creating database models, API endpoints, and frontend forms that integrate with your existing authentication system.

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. The user provided multiple complex features to implement in a comma separated list
2. The todo list helps organize these large features into manageable tasks
3. This approach allows for tracking progress across the entire implementation
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Can you help optimize my React application? It's rendering slowly and has performance issues.</user>
Assistant: I'll help optimize your React application. First, let me examine your codebase to identify potential performance bottlenecks.
*Reviews component structure, render patterns, state management, and data fetching*
Assistant: After analyzing your codebase, I've identified several performance issues. Let me create a todo list to track our optimization efforts.
*Creates todo list with items like: 1) Implement memoization for expensive calculations in ProductList, 2) Add virtualization for long lists in Dashboard, 3) Optimize image loading in Gallery component, 4) Fix state update loops in ShoppingCart, 5) Review bundle size and implement code splitting*
Let's start by implementing memoization for the expensive calculations in your ProductList component.</assistant>

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. First, the assistant examined the codebase to identify specific performance issues
2. Based on this analysis, it identified multiple optimization opportunities
3. Performance optimization is a non-trivial task requiring multiple steps
4. The todo list helps methodically track improvements across different components
5. This systematic approach ensures all performance bottlenecks are addressed
</reasoning>
</example>

## Examples of When NOT to Use the Todo List

<example>
User: How do I print 'Hello World' in Python?
Assistant: In Python, you can print "Hello World" with this simple code:

python
print("Hello World")

This will output the text "Hello World" to the console when executed.</assistant>

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single, trivial task that can be completed in one step. There's no need to track multiple tasks or steps for such a straightforward request.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: What does the git status command do?
Assistant: The git status command shows the current state of your working directory and staging area. It displays which changes have been staged, which haven't, and which files aren't being tracked by Git. This is a fundamental command for understanding the current state of your repository before making commits.

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is an informational request with no actual coding task to complete. The user is simply asking for an explanation, not for the assistant to perform multiple steps or tasks.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Can you add a comment to the calculateTotal function to explain what it does?
Assistant: Sure, let me add a comment to the calculateTotal function to explain what it does.
* Uses the Edit tool to add a comment to the calculateTotal function *

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single, straightforward task confined to one location in the code. Adding a comment doesn't require tracking multiple steps or systematic organization.
</reasoning>

User: Run npm install for me and tell me what happens.
Assistant: I'll run the npm install command for you.

*Executes: npm install*

The command completed successfully. Here's the output:

All dependencies have been installed according to your package.json file.

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single command execution with immediate results. There are no multiple steps to track or organize, making the todo list unnecessary for this straightforward task.
</reasoning>
</example>

## Task States and Management

1. **Task States**: Use these states to track progress:
   - pending: Task not yet started
   - in_progress: Currently working on (limit to ONE task at a time)
   - completed: Task finished successfully
   - cancelled: Task no longer needed

2. **Task Management**:
   - Update task status in real-time as you work
   - Mark tasks complete IMMEDIATELY after finishing (don't batch completions)
   - Only have ONE task in_progress at any time
   - Complete current tasks before starting new ones
   - Cancel tasks that become irrelevant

3. **Task Breakdown**:
   - Create specific, actionable items
   - Break complex tasks into smaller, manageable steps
   - Use clear, descriptive task names

When in doubt, use this tool. Being proactive with task management demonstrates attentiveness and ensures you complete all requirements successfully.

data.content:
# Write Cypher

## Overview

Use this skill for VAST DB Cypher query patterns. Prefer reusable templates, and construct new queries when no template matches the analysis need.

## Workflow

1. Read `references/query-templates.md`. If a template matches the task, reuse it as-is.
2. If no template matches, construct a new Cypher query using the VAST DB schema and the closest related template as guidance.

## Query Strategy

- Load `understand-mlir-schema` when query construction or result interpretation depends on schema details.
- Use small exploratory queries with `LIMIT` when schema details such as operation names, properties, node and relationship labels are uncertain.
- When starting from a source location, first map it to operation `uid`s. Use those `uid`s as stable handles for follow-up queries such as enclosing functions, call relationships, and def-use data flow.
- Return only properties needed for the analysis. Common fields are node `uid`, relationship `idx`, operation `name`, `location`, `func_name`, `fs_raw_name`, `varName`, `className`, type `type`, and attribute `value`.
- Order indexed relationships by `idx`.

## Query Rules

- Generate read-only queries by default. Do not use `CREATE`, `MERGE`, `SET`, `DELETE`, or `REMOVE`.
- Use parameter placeholders such as `$func_name`, `$op_uid`, `$file_name`, `$line`, and `$callee`.
- Do not invent node and relationship labels, properties, or operation names. Verify unfamiliar schema details with `understand-mlir-schema` or exploratory queries.

## Common Patterns

Read `references/query-templates.md` for reusable Cypher templates.

Use this before writing Cypher queries against VAST DB. For query patterns, load write-cypher.

# Understand MLIR Schema

## Overview

This skill describes the VAST DB schema. Use it to check node labels, relationship types, and properties before writing or interpreting Cypher queries. Do not guess labels or relationships; look them up here.

## Core Schema

### Node Labels

- `OPERATIONS` — MLIR operations. Properties include `uid`, `name`, `func_name`, `fs_raw_name`, `varName`, `mod_name`, and `location`. The `location` field has the form `['F', file, line, column]`.
- `VALUES` — SSA values. Only property is `uid`.
- `INTATTRS` — Integer attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (int), `attr_type` (int).
- `FLOATATTRS` — Float attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (float), `attr_type` (int).
- `STRINGATTRS` — String attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (string), `attr_type` (int).
- `TYPES` — MLIR types. Properties: `uid`, `type` (type mnemonic), `has_dl` (bool), `bw` (int bitwidth), `align` (int), `record_name` (string, for hl.record only).
- `REGIONS` — MLIR regions. Properties: `uid`, `regType` (bool: true for GraphRegion, false for SSACFG).
- `BLOCKS` — MLIR blocks. Only property is `uid`.
- `VTABLES` — Virtual tables. Properties: `uid`, `vtable_name`, `components_flat`, `components_sizes`.
- `VTTS` — VTT (virtual table table). Properties: `uid`, `vtt_name`, `components_flat`.

### Key Relationships

Operation operands and results:
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVALUE {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — operation consumes a value as operand
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:RESVALUE {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — operation produces a value as result
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGATTR {idx}]->(:INTATTRS|FLOATATTRS|STRINGATTRS)` — operation has an attribute
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGBLOCK {idx}]->(:BLOCKS)` — operation references a block (e.g., branch targets)
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGTYPE {idx}]->(:TYPES)` — operation has a type operand

Containment (operation bodies):
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:CHILDREGION {idx}]->(:REGIONS)` — operation contains a region
- `(:REGIONS)-[:CHILDBLOCK {idx}]->(:BLOCKS)` — region contains a block
- `(:BLOCKS)-[:CHILDINST {idx}]->(:OPERATIONS)` — block contains an instruction (operation)
- `(:BLOCKS)-[:BLOCKARG {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — block has a block argument

Type system:
- `(:VALUES)-[:TYPE]->(:TYPES)` — value has a type
- `(:TYPES)-[:ARG {idx}]->(:TYPES)` — composite type has component types
- `(:INTATTRS|:FLOATATTRS)-[:TYPE]->(:TYPES)` — non-string attribute has a type

Function and variable types:
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:FUNCTYPE]->(:TYPES)` — global function's type signature
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:VARTYPE]->(:TYPES)` — global variable's type

Virtual tables (only from `builtin.module` operations):
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVTABLE]->(:VTABLES)` — module has a vtable
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVTT]->(:VTTS)` — module has a VTT

## References

- `references/schema-mlir-graph.md` — canonical node labels, relationships, properties, constraints, and example queries.
- `references/operations-catalog.md` — every operation name (`hl.func`, `hl.call`, `ll.load`, etc.) with its specific properties and relationships.
- `references/types-catalog.md` — every type mnemonic (`hl.int`, `i32`, `core.fn`, etc.) with its properties and subtype structure.

Based on my analysis, I can now provide the explanation:

## Root Cause

The vulnerability occurs in `close_buffer()` at line 1086-1087. The function receives a `win` parameter that can be NULL (passed from `set_curbuf()` at line 1194 via the ternary `prevbuf == curwin->w_buffer ? curwin : NULL`). At line 1086, the code calls `win_valid_any_tab(win)` **without first checking if `win` is NULL**. If `win_valid_any_tab(NULL)` returns true (non-zero), the short-circuit evaluation proceeds to dereference `win->w_buffer` at line 1087, causing a null pointer dereference (CWE-476).

## Trigger Path

1. `bad.c:1222` `do_buffer(int action, int start, int dir, int count, int forceit)`
   - Entry point for buffer operations; eventually needs to switch to a different buffer

2. `bad.c:1549` `set_curbuf(buf, action)`
   - Called to switch current buffer; saves previous buffer in `prevbuf` variable

3. `bad.c:1194` `close_buffer(prevbuf == curwin->w_buffer ? curwin : NULL, prevbuf, ...)`
   - Ternary passes `NULL` when `prevbuf` differs from current window's buffer, intended to indicate "no window owns this buffer"

4. `bad.c:1086` `if (win_valid_any_tab(win) && win->w_buffer == buf)`
   - Missing NULL check on `win` before calling `win_valid_any_tab()`; if it returns true, proceeds to dereference

Based on my code analysis, I now have enough information. The VAST DB has limited line-level data but confirmed the function locations. Let me provide my analysis:

## Root Cause

The vulnerability is a CWE-476 Null Pointer Dereference in Vim's buffer management code. In `set_curbuf()` at line 1194, `close_buffer()` is called with either `curwin` or `NULL` as the first argument depending on whether `prevbuf == curwin->w_buffer`. When `prevbuf` differs from the current window's buffer, `NULL` is passed as the window parameter. Inside `close_buffer()` at line 1086-1087, the code attempts `win->w_buffer = NULL` without adequate NULL validation, causing a null pointer dereference if `win_valid_any_tab(win)` incorrectly returns TRUE for NULL or if autocommands alter window state between the check and the assignment.

## Trigger Path

1. `bad.c:794` `set_curbuf`
   - Called to switch to a different buffer. At line 1164, captures `prevbuf = curbuf`. At line 1194, calls `close_buffer()` with either `curwin` or `NULL` depending on buffer comparison.

2. `bad.c:789` `close_buffer`
   - Entry point for buffer closing. At line 1086-1087, if `win_valid_any_tab(win)` passes and `win->w_buffer == buf`, it sets `win->w_buffer = NULL`. When `win` is NULL from the caller, this triggers the null pointer dereference.

879:     void
880: close_buffer(
881:     win_T	*win,		// if not NULL, set b_last_cursor
882:     buf_T	*buf,
883:     int		action,
884:     int		abort_if_last,
885:     int		ignore_abort)
886: {
887:     int		is_curbuf;
888:     int		nwindows;
889:     bufref_T	bufref;
890:     int		is_curwin = (curwin != NULL && curwin->w_buffer == buf);
891:     win_T	*the_curwin = curwin;
892:     tabpage_T	*the_curtab = curtab;
893:     int		unload_buf = (action != 0);
894:     int		wipe_buf = (action == DOBUF_WIPE || action == DOBUF_WIPE_REUSE);
895:     int		del_buf = (action == DOBUF_DEL || wipe_buf);
896: 
897:     CHECK_CURBUF;
898:     /*
899:      * Force unloading or deleting when 'bufhidden' says so.
900:      * The caller must take care of NOT deleting/freeing when 'bufhidden' is
901:      * "hide" (otherwise we could never free or delete a buffer).
902:      */
903:     if (buf->b_p_bh[0] == 'd')		// 'bufhidden' == "delete"
904:     {
905: 	del_buf = TRUE;
906: 	unload_buf = TRUE;
907:     }
908:     else if (buf->b_p_bh[0] == 'w')	// 'bufhidden' == "wipe"
909:     {
910: 	del_buf = TRUE;
911: 	unload_buf = TRUE;
912: 	wipe_buf = TRUE;
913:     }
914:     else if (buf->b_p_bh[0] == 'u')	// 'bufhidden' == "unload"
915: 	unload_buf = TRUE;
916: 
917: #ifdef FEAT_TERMINAL
918:     if (bt_terminal(buf) && (buf->b_nwindows == 1 || del_buf))
919:     {
920: 	CHECK_CURBUF;
921: 	if (term_job_running(buf->b_term))
922: 	{
923: 	    if (wipe_buf || unload_buf)
924: 	    {
925: 		if (!can_unload_buffer(buf))
926: 		    return;
927: 
928: 		// Wiping out or unloading a terminal buffer kills the job.
929: 		free_terminal(buf);
930: 	    }
931: 	    else
932: 	    {
933: 		// The job keeps running, hide the buffer.
934: 		del_buf = FALSE;
935: 		unload_buf = FALSE;
936: 	    }
937: 	}
938: 	else if (buf->b_p_bh[0] == 'h' && !del_buf)
939: 	{
940: 	    // Hide a terminal buffer.
941: 	    unload_buf = FALSE;
942: 	}
943: 	else
944: 	{
945: 	    // A terminal buffer is wiped out if the job has finished.
946: 	    del_buf = TRUE;
947: 	    unload_buf = TRUE;
948: 	    wipe_buf = TRUE;
949: 	}
950: 	CHECK_CURBUF;
951:     }
952: #endif
953: 
954:     // Disallow deleting the buffer when it is locked (already being closed or
955:     // halfway a command that relies on it). Unloading is allowed.
956:     if ((del_buf || wipe_buf) && !can_unload_buffer(buf))
957: 	return;
958: 
959:     // check no autocommands closed the window
960:     if (win != NULL && win_valid_any_tab(win))
961:     {
962: 	// Set b_last_cursor when closing the last window for the buffer.
963: 	// Remember the last cursor position and window options of the buffer.
964: 	// This used to be only for the current window, but then options like
965: 	// 'foldmethod' may be lost with a ":only" command.
966: 	if (buf->b_nwindows == 1)
967: 	    set_last_cursor(win);
968: 	buflist_setfpos(buf, win,
969: 			win->w_cursor.lnum == 1 ? 0 : win->w_cursor.lnum,
970: 			win->w_cursor.col, TRUE);
971:     }
972: 
973:     set_bufref(&bufref, buf);
974: 
975:     // When the buffer is no longer in a window, trigger BufWinLeave
976:     if (buf->b_nwindows == 1)
977:     {
978: 	++buf->b_locked;
979: 	if (apply_autocmds(EVENT_BUFWINLEAVE, buf->b_fname, buf->b_fname,
980: 								  FALSE, buf)
981: 		&& !bufref_valid(&bufref))
982: 	{
983: 	    // Autocommands deleted the buffer.
984: aucmd_abort:
985: 	    emsg(_(e_auabort));
986: 	    return;
987: 	}
988: 	--buf->b_locked;
989: 	if (abort_if_last && one_window())
990: 	    // Autocommands made this the only window.
991: 	    goto aucmd_abort;
992: 
993: 	// When the buffer becomes hidden, but is not unloaded, trigger
994: 	// BufHidden
995: 	if (!unload_buf)
996: 	{
997: 	    ++buf->b_locked;
998: 	    if (apply_autocmds(EVENT_BUFHIDDEN, buf->b_fname, buf->b_fname,
999: 								  FALSE, buf)
1000: 		    && !bufref_valid(&bufref))
1001: 		// Autocommands deleted the buffer.
1002: 		goto aucmd_abort;
1003: 	    --buf->b_locked;
1004: 	    if (abort_if_last && one_window())
1005: 		// Autocommands made this the only window.
1006: 		goto aucmd_abort;
1007: 	}
1008: #ifdef FEAT_EVAL
1009: 	// autocmds may abort script processing
1010: 	if (!ignore_abort && aborting())
1011: 	    return;
1012: #endif
1013:     }
1014: 
1015:     // If the buffer was in curwin and the window has changed, go back to that
1016:     // window, if it still exists.  This avoids that ":edit x" triggering a
1017:     // "tabnext" BufUnload autocmd leaves a window behind without a buffer.
1018:     if (is_curwin && curwin != the_curwin &&  win_valid_any_tab(the_curwin))
1019:     {
1020: 	block_autocmds();
1021: 	goto_tabpage_win(the_curtab, the_curwin);
1022: 	unblock_autocmds();
1023:     }
1024: 
1025:     nwindows = buf->b_nwindows;
1026: 
1027:     // decrease the link count from windows (unless not in any window)
1028:     if (buf->b_nwindows > 0)
1029: 	--buf->b_nwindows;
1030: 
1031: #ifdef FEAT_DIFF
1032:     if (diffopt_hiddenoff() && !unload_buf && buf->b_nwindows == 0)
1033: 	diff_buf_delete(buf);	// Clear 'diff' for hidden buffer.
1034: #endif
1035: 
1036:     // Return when a window is displaying the buffer or when it's not
1037:     // unloaded.
1038:     if (buf->b_nwindows > 0 || !unload_buf)
1039: 	return;
1040: 
1041:     // Always remove the buffer when there is no file name.
1042:     if (buf->b_ffname == NULL)
1043: 	del_buf = TRUE;
1044: 
1045:     // When closing the current buffer stop Visual mode before freeing
1046:     // anything.
1047:     if (buf == curbuf && VIsual_active
1048: #if defined(EXITFREE)
1049: 	    && !entered_free_all_mem
1050: #endif
1051: 	    )
1052: 	end_visual_mode();
1053: 
1054:     /*
1055:      * Free all things allocated for this buffer.
1056:      * Also calls the "BufDelete" autocommands when del_buf is TRUE.
1057:      */
1058:     // Remember if we are closing the current buffer.  Restore the number of
1059:     // windows, so that autocommands in buf_freeall() don't get confused.
1060:     is_curbuf = (buf == curbuf);
1061:     buf->b_nwindows = nwindows;
1062: 
1063:     buf_freeall(buf, (del_buf ? BFA_DEL : 0)
1064: 		       + (wipe_buf ? BFA_WIPE : 0)
1065: 		       + (ignore_abort ? BFA_IGNORE_ABORT : 0));
1066: 
1067:     // Autocommands may have deleted the buffer.
1068:     if (!bufref_valid(&bufref))
1069: 	return;
1070: #ifdef FEAT_EVAL
1071:     // autocmds may abort script processing
1072:     if (!ignore_abort && aborting())
1073: 	return;
1074: #endif
1075: 
1076:     /*
1077:      * It's possible that autocommands change curbuf to the one being deleted.
1078:      * This might cause the previous curbuf to be deleted unexpectedly.  But
1079:      * in some cases it's OK to delete the curbuf, because a new one is
1080:      * obtained anyway.  Therefore only return if curbuf changed to the
1081:      * deleted buffer.
1082:      */
1083:     if (buf == curbuf && !is_curbuf)
1084: 	return;
1085: 
1086:     if (win_valid_any_tab(win) && win->w_buffer == buf)
1087: 	win->w_buffer = NULL;  // make sure we don't use the buffer now
1088: 
1089:     // Autocommands may have opened or closed windows for this buffer.
1090:     // Decrement the count for the close we do here.
1091:     if (buf->b_nwindows > 0)
1092: 	--buf->b_nwindows;
1093: 
1094:     /*
1095:      * Remove the buffer from the list.
1096:      */
1097:     if (wipe_buf)
1098:     {
1099: 	if (action == DOBUF_WIPE_REUSE)
1100: 	{
1101: 	    // we can re-use this buffer number, store it
1102: 	    if (buf_reuse.ga_itemsize == 0)
1103: 		ga_init2(&buf_reuse, sizeof(int), 50);
1104: 	    if (ga_grow(&buf_reuse, 1) == OK)
1105: 		((int *)buf_reuse.ga_data)[buf_reuse.ga_len++] = buf->b_fnum;
1106: 	}
1107: 	if (buf->b_sfname != buf->b_ffname)
1108: 	    VIM_CLEAR(buf->b_sfname);
1109: 	else
1110: 	    buf->b_sfname = NULL;
1111: 	VIM_CLEAR(buf->b_ffname);
1112: 	if (buf->b_prev == NULL)
1113: 	    firstbuf = buf->b_next;
1114: 	else
1115: 	    buf->b_prev->b_next = buf->b_next;
1116: 	if (buf->b_next == NULL)
1117: 	    lastbuf = buf->b_prev;
1118: 	else
1119: 	    buf->b_next->b_prev = buf->b_prev;
1120: 	free_buffer(buf);
1121:     }
1122:     else
1123:     {
1124: 	if (del_buf)
1125: 	{
1126: 	    // Free all internal variables and reset option values, to make
1127: 	    // ":bdel" compatible with Vim 5.7.
1128: 	    free_buffer_stuff(buf, TRUE);
1129: 
1130: 	    // Make it look like a new buffer.
1131: 	    buf->b_flags = BF_CHECK_RO | BF_NEVERLOADED;
1132: 
1133: 	    // Init the options when loaded again.
1134: 	    buf->b_p_initialized = FALSE;
1135: 	}
1136: 	buf_clear_file(buf);
1137: 	if (del_buf)
1138: 	    buf->b_p_bl = FALSE;
1139:     }
1140:     // NOTE: at this point "curbuf" may be invalid!
1141: }

1143:     void
1144: set_curbuf(buf_T *buf, int action)
1145: {
1146:     buf_T	*prevbuf;
1147:     int		unload = (action == DOBUF_UNLOAD || action == DOBUF_DEL
1148: 			|| action == DOBUF_WIPE || action == DOBUF_WIPE_REUSE);
1149: #ifdef FEAT_SYN_HL
1150:     long	old_tw = curbuf->b_p_tw;
1151: #endif
1152:     bufref_T	newbufref;
1153:     bufref_T	prevbufref;
1154: 
1155:     setpcmark();
1156:     if ((cmdmod.cmod_flags & CMOD_KEEPALT) == 0)
1157: 	curwin->w_alt_fnum = curbuf->b_fnum; // remember alternate file
1158:     buflist_altfpos(curwin);			 // remember curpos
1159: 
1160:     // Don't restart Select mode after switching to another buffer.
1161:     VIsual_reselect = FALSE;
1162: 
1163:     // close_windows() or apply_autocmds() may change curbuf and wipe out "buf"
1164:     prevbuf = curbuf;
1165:     set_bufref(&prevbufref, prevbuf);
1166:     set_bufref(&newbufref, buf);
1167: 
1168:     // Autocommands may delete the current buffer and/or the buffer we want to go
1169:     // to.  In those cases don't close the buffer.
1170:     if (!apply_autocmds(EVENT_BUFLEAVE, NULL, NULL, FALSE, curbuf)
1171: 	    || (bufref_valid(&prevbufref)
1172: 		&& bufref_valid(&newbufref)
1173: #ifdef FEAT_EVAL
1174: 		&& !aborting()
1175: #endif
1176: 	       ))
1177:     {
1178: #ifdef FEAT_SYN_HL
1179: 	if (prevbuf == curwin->w_buffer)
1180: 	    reset_synblock(curwin);
1181: #endif
1182: 	if (unload)
1183: 	    close_windows(prevbuf, FALSE);
1184: #if defined(FEAT_EVAL)
1185: 	if (bufref_valid(&prevbufref) && !aborting())
1186: #else
1187: 	if (bufref_valid(&prevbufref))
1188: #endif
1189: 	{
1190: 	    win_T  *previouswin = curwin;
1191: 
1192: 	    if (prevbuf == curbuf)
1193: 		u_sync(FALSE);
1194: 	    close_buffer(prevbuf == curwin->w_buffer ? curwin : NULL, prevbuf,
1195: 		    unload ? action : (action == DOBUF_GOTO
1196: 			&& !buf_hide(prevbuf)
1197: 			&& !bufIsChanged(prevbuf)) ? DOBUF_UNLOAD : 0,
1198: 		    FALSE, FALSE);
1199: 	    if (curwin != previouswin && win_valid(previouswin))
1200: 	      // autocommands changed curwin, Grr!
1201: 	      curwin = previouswin;
1202: 	}
1203:     }
1204:     // An autocommand may have deleted "buf", already entered it (e.g., when
1205:     // it did ":bunload") or aborted the script processing.
1206:     // If curwin->w_buffer is null, enter_buffer() will make it valid again
1207:     if ((buf_valid(buf) && buf != curbuf
1208: #ifdef FEAT_EVAL
1209: 		&& !aborting()
1210: #endif
1211: 	) || curwin->w_buffer == NULL)
1212:     {
1213: 	enter_buffer(buf);
1214: #ifdef FEAT_SYN_HL
1215: 	if (old_tw != curbuf->b_p_tw)
1216: 	    check_colorcolumn(curwin);
1217: #endif
1218:     }
1219: }

1221:     int
1222: do_buffer(
1223:     int		action,
1224:     int		start,
1225:     int		dir,		// FORWARD or BACKWARD
1226:     int		count,		// buffer number or number of buffers
1227:     int		forceit)	// TRUE for :...!
1228: {
1229:     buf_T	*buf;
1230:     buf_T	*bp;
1231:     int		unload = (action == DOBUF_UNLOAD || action == DOBUF_DEL
1232: 			|| action == DOBUF_WIPE || action == DOBUF_WIPE_REUSE);
1233:

899:      * Force unloading or deleting when 'bufhidden' says so.
900:      * The caller must take care of NOT deleting/freeing when 'bufhidden' is
901:      * "hide" (otherwise we could never free or delete a buffer).
902:      */
903:     if (buf->b_p_bh[0] == 'd')		// 'bufhidden' == "delete"
904:     {
905: 	del_buf = TRUE;
906: 	unload_buf = TRUE;
907:     }
908:     else if (buf->b_p_bh[0] == 'w')	// 'bufhidden' == "wipe"
909:     {
910: 	del_buf = TRUE;
911: 	unload_buf = TRUE;
912: 	wipe_buf = TRUE;
913:     }
914:     else if (buf->b_p_bh[0] == 'u')	// 'bufhidden' == "unload"
915: 	unload_buf = TRUE;
916: 
917: #ifdef FEAT_TERMINAL
918:     if (bt_terminal(buf) && (buf->b_nwindows == 1 || del_buf))
919:     {
920: 	CHECK_CURBUF;
921: 	if (term_job_running(buf->b_term))
922: 	{
923: 	    if (wipe_buf || unload_buf)
924: 	    {
925: 		if (!can_unload_buffer(buf))
926: 		    return FALSE;
927: 
928: 		// Wiping out or unloading a terminal buffer kills the job.
929: 		free_terminal(buf);
930: 	    }
931: 	    else
932: 	    {
933: 		// The job keeps running, hide the buffer.
934: 		del_buf = FALSE;
935: 		unload_buf = FALSE;
936: 	    }
937: 	}
938: 	else if (buf->b_p_bh[0] == 'h' && !del_buf)
939: 	{
940: 	    // Hide a terminal buffer.
941: 	    unload_buf = FALSE;
942: 	}
943: 	else
944: 	{
945: 	    // A terminal buffer is wiped out if the job has finished.
946: 	    del_buf = TRUE;
947: 	    unload_buf = TRUE;
948: 	    wipe_buf = TRUE;
949: 	}
950: 	CHECK_CURBUF;
951:     }
952: #endif
953: 
954:     // Disallow deleting the buffer when it is locked (already being closed or
955:     // halfway a command that relies on it). Unloading is allowed.
956:     if ((del_buf || wipe_buf) && !can_unload_buffer(buf))
957: 	return FALSE;
958: 
959:     // check no autocommands closed the window
960:     if (win != NULL && win_valid_any_tab(win))
961:     {
962: 	// Set b_last_cursor when closing the last window for the buffer.
963: 	// Remember the last cursor position and window options of the buffer.
964: 	// This used to be only for the current window, but then options like
965: 	// 'foldmethod' may be lost with a ":only" command.
966: 	if (buf->b_nwindows == 1)
967: 	    set_last_cursor(win);
968: 	buflist_setfpos(buf, win,
969: 			win->w_cursor.lnum == 1 ? 0 : win->w_cursor.lnum,
970: 			win->w_cursor.col, TRUE);
971:     }
972: 
973:     set_bufref(&bufref, buf);
974: 
975:     // When the buffer is no longer in a window, trigger BufWinLeave
976:     if (buf->b_nwindows == 1)
977:     {
978: 	++buf->b_locked;
979: 	if (apply_autocmds(EVENT_BUFWINLEAVE, buf->b_fname, buf->b_fname,
980: 								  FALSE, buf)
981: 		&& !bufref_valid(&bufref))
982: 	{
983: 	    // Autocommands deleted the buffer.
984: aucmd_abort:
985: 	    emsg(_(e_auabort));
986: 	    return FALSE;
987: 	}
988: 	--buf->b_locked;
989: 	if (abort_if_last && one_window())
990: 	    // Autocommands made this the only window.
991: 	    goto aucmd_abort;
992: 
993: 	// When the buffer becomes hidden, but is not unloaded, trigger
994: 	// BufHidden
995: 	if (!unload_buf)
996: 	{
997: 	    ++buf->b_locked;
998: 	    if (apply_autocmds(EVENT_BUFHIDDEN, buf->b_fname, buf->b_fname,
999: 								  FALSE, buf)
1000: 		    && !bufref_valid(&bufref))
1001: 		// Autocommands deleted the buffer.
1002: 		goto aucmd_abort;
1003: 	    --buf->b_locked;
1004: 	    if (abort_if_last && one_window())
1005: 		// Autocommands made this the only window.
1006: 		goto aucmd_abort;
1007: 	}
1008: #ifdef FEAT_EVAL
1009: 	// autocmds may abort script processing
1010: 	if (!ignore_abort && aborting())
1011: 	    return FALSE;
1012: #endif
1013:     }
1014: 
1015:     // If the buffer was in curwin and the window has changed, go back to that
1016:     // window, if it still exists.  This avoids that ":edit x" triggering a
1017:     // "tabnext" BufUnload autocmd leaves a window behind without a buffer.
1018:     if (is_curwin && curwin != the_curwin &&  win_valid_any_tab(the_curwin))
1019:     {
1020: 	block_autocmds();
1021: 	goto_tabpage_win(the_curtab, the_curwin);
1022: 	unblock_autocmds();
1023:     }
1024: 
1025:     nwindows = buf->b_nwindows;
1026: 
1027:     // decrease the link count from windows (unless not in any window)
1028:     if (buf->b_nwindows > 0)
1029: 	--buf->b_nwindows;
1030: 
1031: #ifdef FEAT_DIFF
1032:     if (diffopt_hiddenoff() && !unload_buf && buf->b_nwindows == 0)
1033: 	diff_buf_delete(buf);	// Clear 'diff' for hidden buffer.
1034: #endif
1035: 
1036:     // Return when a window is displaying the buffer or when it's not
1037:     // unloaded.
1038:     if (buf->b_nwindows > 0 || !unload_buf)
1039: 	return FALSE;
1040: 
1041:     // Always remove the buffer when there is no file name.
1042:     if (buf->b_ffname == NULL)
1043: 	del_buf = TRUE;
1044: 
1045:     // When closing the current buffer stop Visual mode before freeing
1046:     // anything.
1047:     if (buf == curbuf && VIsual_active
1048: #if defined(EXITFREE)
1049: 	    && !entered_free_all_mem
1050: #endif
1051: 	    )
1052: 	end_visual_mode();
1053: 
1054:     /*
1055:      * Free all things allocated for this buffer.
1056:      * Also calls the "BufDelete" autocommands when del_buf is TRUE.
1057:      */
1058:     // Remember if we are closing the current buffer.  Restore the number of
1059:     // windows, so that autocommands in buf_freeall() don't get confused.
1060:     is_curbuf = (buf == curbuf);
1061:     buf->b_nwindows = nwindows;
1062: 
1063:     buf_freeall(buf, (del_buf ? BFA_DEL : 0)
1064: 		       + (wipe_buf ? BFA_WIPE : 0)
1065: 		       + (ignore_abort ? BFA_IGNORE_ABORT : 0));
1066: 
1067:     // Autocommands may have deleted the buffer.
1068:     if (!bufref_valid(&bufref))
1069: 	return FALSE;
1070: #ifdef FEAT_EVAL
1071:     // autocmds may abort script processing
1072:     if (!ignore_abort && aborting())
1073: 	return FALSE;
1074: #endif
1075: 
1076:     /*
1077:      * It's possible that autocommands change curbuf to the one being deleted.
1078:      * This might cause the previous curbuf to be deleted unexpectedly.  But
1079:      * in some cases it's OK to delete the curbuf, because a new one is
1080:      * obtained anyway.  Therefore only return if curbuf changed to the
1081:      * deleted buffer.
1082:      */
1083:     if (buf == curbuf && !is_curbuf)
1084: 	return FALSE;
1085: 
1086:     if (win_valid_any_tab(win) && win->w_buffer == buf)
1087: 	win->w_buffer = NULL;  // make sure we don't use the buffer now
1088: 
1089:     // Autocommands may have opened or closed windows for this buffer.
1090:     // Decrement the count for the close we do here.
1091:     if (buf->b_nwindows > 0)
1092: 	--buf->b_nwindows;
1093: 
1094:     /*
1095:      * Remove the buffer from the list.
1096:      */
1097:     if (wipe_buf)
1098:     {
1099: 	if (action == DOBUF_WIPE_REUSE)
1100: 	{
1101: 	    // we can re-use this buffer number, store it
1102: 	    if (buf_reuse.ga_itemsize == 0)
1103: 		ga_init2(&buf_reuse, sizeof(int), 50);
1104: 	    if (ga_grow(&buf_reuse, 1) == OK)
1105: 		((int *)buf_reuse.ga_data)[buf_reuse.ga_len++] = buf->b_fnum;
1106: 	}
1107: 	if (buf->b_sfname != buf->b_ffname)
1108: 	    VIM_CLEAR(buf->b_sfname);
1109: 	else
1110: 	    buf->b_sfname = NULL;
1111: 	VIM_CLEAR(buf->b_ffname);
1112: 	if (buf->b_prev == NULL)
1113: 	    firstbuf = buf->b_next;
1114: 	else
1115: 	    buf->b_prev->b_next = buf->b_next;
1116: 	if (buf->b_next == NULL)
1117: 	    lastbuf = buf->b_prev;
1118: 	else
1119: 	    buf->b_next->b_prev = buf->b_prev;
1120: 	free_buffer(buf);
1121:     }
1122:     else
1123:     {
1124: 	if (del_buf)
1125: 	{
1126: 	    // Free all internal variables and reset option values, to make
1127: 	    // ":bdel" compatible with Vim 5.7.
1128: 	    free_buffer_stuff(buf, TRUE);
1129: 
1130: 	    // Make it look like a new buffer.
1131: 	    buf->b_flags = BF_CHECK_RO | BF_NEVERLOADED;
1132: 
1133: 	    // Init the options when loaded again.
1134: 	    buf->b_p_initialized = FALSE;
1135: 	}
1136: 	buf_clear_file(buf);
1137: 	if (del_buf)
1138: 	    buf->b_p_bl = FALSE;
1139:     }
1140:     // NOTE: at this point "curbuf" may be invalid!
1141:     return TRUE;
1142: }

1144:     void
1145: set_curbuf(buf_T *buf, int action)
1146: {
1147:     buf_T	*prevbuf;
1148:     int		unload = (action == DOBUF_UNLOAD || action == DOBUF_DEL
1149: 			|| action == DOBUF_WIPE || action == DOBUF_WIPE_REUSE);
1150: #ifdef FEAT_SYN_HL
1151:     long	old_tw = curbuf->b_p_tw;
1152: #endif
1153:     bufref_T	newbufref;
1154:     bufref_T	prevbufref;
1155: 
1156:     setpcmark();
1157:     if ((cmdmod.cmod_flags & CMOD_KEEPALT) == 0)
1158: 	curwin->w_alt_fnum = curbuf->b_fnum; // remember alternate file
1159:     buflist_altfpos(curwin);			 // remember curpos
1160: 
1161:     // Don't restart Select mode after switching to another buffer.
1162:     VIsual_reselect = FALSE;
1163: 
1164:     // close_windows() or apply_autocmds() may change curbuf and wipe out "buf"
1165:     prevbuf = curbuf;
1166:     set_bufref(&prevbufref, prevbuf);
1167:     set_bufref(&newbufref, buf);
1168: 
1169:     // Autocommands may delete the current buffer and/or the buffer we want to go
1170:     // to.  In those cases don't close the buffer.
1171:     if (!apply_autocmds(EVENT_BUFLEAVE, NULL, NULL, FALSE, curbuf)
1172: 	    || (bufref_valid(&prevbufref)
1173: 		&& bufref_valid(&newbufref)
1174: #ifdef FEAT_EVAL
1175: 		&& !aborting()
1176: #endif
1177: 	       ))
1178:     {
1179: #ifdef FEAT_SYN_HL
1180: 	if (prevbuf == curwin->w_buffer)
1181: 	    reset_synblock(curwin);
1182: #endif
1183: 	if (unload)
1184: 	    close_windows(prevbuf, FALSE);
1185: #if defined(FEAT_EVAL)
1186: 	if (bufref_valid(&prevbufref) && !aborting())
1187: #else
1188: 	if (bufref_valid(&prevbufref))
1189: #endif
1190: 	{
1191: 	    win_T  *previouswin = curwin;
1192: 
1193: 	    if (prevbuf == curbuf)
1194: 		u_sync(FALSE);
1195: 	    close_buffer(prevbuf == curwin->w_buffer ? curwin : NULL, prevbuf,
1196: 		    unload ? action : (action == DOBUF_GOTO
1197: 			&& !buf_hide(prevbuf)
1198: 			&& !bufIsChanged(prevbuf)) ? DOBUF_UNLOAD : 0,
1199: 		    FALSE, FALSE);
1200: 	    if (curwin != previouswin && win_valid(previouswin))
1201: 	      // autocommands changed curwin, Grr!
1202: 	      curwin = previouswin;
1203: 	}
1204:     }
1205:     // An autocommand may have deleted "buf", already entered it (e.g., when
1206:     // it did ":bunload") or aborted the script processing.
1207:     // If curwin->w_buffer is null, enter_buffer() will make it valid again
1208:     if ((buf_valid(buf) && buf != curbuf
1209: #ifdef FEAT_EVAL
1210: 		&& !aborting()
1211: #endif
1212: 	) || curwin->w_buffer == NULL)
1213:     {
1214: 	enter_buffer(buf);
1215: #ifdef FEAT_SYN_HL
1216: 	if (old_tw != curbuf->b_p_tw)
1217: 	    check_colorcolumn(curwin);
1218: #endif
1219:     }
1220: }

1222:     int
1223: do_buffer(
1224:     int		action,
1225:     int		start,
1226:     int		dir,		// FORWARD or BACKWARD
1227:     int		count,		// buffer number or number of buffers
1228:     int		forceit)	// TRUE for :...!
1229: {
1230:     buf_T	*buf;
1231:     buf_T	*bp;
1232:     int		unload = (action == DOBUF_UNLOAD || action == DOBUF_DEL
1233: 			|| action == DOBUF_WIPE || action == DOBUF_WIPE_REUSE);
1234: 
1235:     switch (start)
1236:     {
1237: 	case DOBUF_FIRST:   buf = firstbuf; break;
1238: 	case DOBUF_LAST:    buf = lastbuf;  break;
1239: 	default:	    buf = curbuf;   break;
1240:     }
1241:     if (start == DOBUF_MOD)	    // find next modified buffer
1242:     {
1243: 	while (count-- > 0)
1244: 	{
1245: 	    do
1246: 	    {
1247: 		buf = buf->b_next;
1248: 		if (buf == NULL)
1249: 		    buf = firstbuf;
1250: 	    }
1251: 	    while (buf != curbuf && !bufIsChanged(buf));
1252: 	}
1253: 	if (!bufIsChanged(buf))
1254: 	{
1255: 	    emsg(_("E84: No modified buffer found"));
1256: 	    return FAIL;
1257: 	}
1258:     }
1259:     else if (start == DOBUF_FIRST && count) // find specified buffer number
1260:     {
1261: 	while (buf != NULL && buf->b_fnum != count)
1262: 	    buf = buf->b_next;
1263:     }
1264:     else
1265:     {
1266: 	bp = NULL;
1267: 	while (count > 0 || (!unload && !buf->b_p_bl && bp != buf))
1268: 	{
1269: 	    // remember the buffer where we start, we come back there when all
1270: 	    // buffers are unlisted.
1271: 	    if (bp == NULL)
1272: 		bp = buf;
1273: 	    if (dir == FORWARD)
1274: 	    {
1275: 		buf = buf->b_next;
1276: 		if (buf == NULL)
1277: 		    buf = firstbuf;
1278: 	    }
1279: 	    else
1280: 	    {
1281: 		buf = buf->b_prev;
1282: 		if (buf == NULL)
1283: 		    buf = lastbuf;
1284: 	    }
1285: 	    // don't count unlisted buffers
1286: 	    if (unload || buf->b_p_bl)
1287: 	    {
1288: 		 --count;
1289: 		 bp = NULL;	// use this buffer as new starting point
1290: 	    }
1291: 	    if (bp == buf)
1292: 	    {
1293: 		// back where we started, didn't find anything.
1294: 		emsg(_("E85: There is no listed buffer"));
1295: 		return FAIL;
1296: 	    }
1297: 	}
1298:     }
1299: 
1300:     if (buf == NULL)	    // could not find it
1301:     {
1302: 	if (start == DOBUF_FIRST)
1303: 	{
1304: 	    // don't warn when deleting
1305: 	    if (!unload)
1306: 		semsg(_(e_nobufnr), count);
1307: 	}
1308: 	else if (dir == FORWARD)
1309: 	    emsg(_("E87: Cannot go beyond last buffer"));
1310: 	else
1311: 	    emsg(_("E88: Cannot go before first buffer"));
1312: 	return FAIL;
1313:     }
1314: 
1315: #ifdef FEAT_GUI
1316:     need_mouse_correct = TRUE;
1317: #endif
1318: 
1319:     /*
1320:      * delete buffer buf from memory and/or the list
1321:      */
1322:     if (unload)
1323:     {
1324: 	int	forward;
1325: 	bufref_T bufref;
1326: 
1327: 	if (!can_unload_buffer(buf))
1328: 	    return FAIL;
1329: 
1330: 	set_bufref(&bufref, buf);
1331: 
1332: 	// When unloading or deleting a buffer that's already unloaded and
1333: 	// unlisted: fail silently.
1334: 	if (action != DOBUF_WIPE && action != DOBUF_WIPE_REUSE
1335: 					   && buf->b_ml.ml_mfp == NULL && !buf->b_p_bl)
1336: 	    return FAIL;
1337: 
1338: 	if (!forceit && bufIsChanged(buf))
1339: 	{
1340: #if defined(FEAT_GUI_DIALOG) || defined(FEAT_CON_DIALOG)
1341: 	    if ((p_confirm || (cmdmod.cmod_flags & CMOD_CONFIRM)) && p_write)
1342: 	    {
1343: 		dialog_changed(buf, FALSE);
1344: 		if (!bufref_valid(&bufref))
1345: 		    // Autocommand deleted buffer, oops! It's not changed
1346: 		    // now.
1347: 		    return FAIL;
1348: 		// If it's still changed fail silently, the dialog already
1349: 		// mentioned why it fails.
1350: 		if (bufIsChanged(buf))
1351: 		    return FAIL;
1352: 	    }
1353: 	    else
1354: #endif
1355: 	    {
1356: 		semsg(_("E89: No write since last change for buffer %d (add ! to override)"),
1357: 								     buf->b_fnum);
1358: 		return FAIL;
1359: 	    }
1360: 	}
1361: 
1362: 	// When closing the current buffer stop Visual mode.
1363: 	if (buf == curbuf && VIsual_active)
1364: 	    end_visual_mode();
1365: 
1366: 	/*
1367: 	 * If deleting the last (listed) buffer, make it empty.
1368: 	 * The last (listed) buffer cannot be unloaded.
1369: 	 */
1370: 	FOR_ALL_BUFFERS(bp)
1371: 	    if (bp->b_p_bl && bp != buf)
1372: 		break;
1373: 	if (bp == NULL && buf == curbuf)
1374: 	    return empty_curbuf(TRUE, forceit, action);
1375: 
1376: 	/*
1377: 	 * If the deleted buffer is the current one, close the current window
1378: 	 * (unless it's the only window).  Repeat this so long as we end up in
1379: 	 * a window with this buffer.
1380: 	 */
1381: 	while (buf == curbuf
1382: 		   && !(curwin->w_closing || curwin->w_buffer->b_locked > 0)
1383: 		   && (!ONE_WINDOW || first_tabpage->tp_next != NULL))
1384: 	{
1385: 	    if (win_close(curwin, FALSE) == FAIL)
1386: 		break;
1387: 	}
1388: 
1389: 	/*
1390: 	 * If the buffer to be deleted is not the current one, delete it here.
1391: 	 */
1392: 	if (buf != curbuf)
1393: 	{
1394: 	    close_windows(buf, FALSE);
1395: 	    if (buf != curbuf && bufref_valid(&bufref) && buf->b_nwindows <= 0)
1396: 		    close_buffer(NULL, buf, action, FALSE, FALSE);
1397: 	    return OK;
1398: 	}
1399: 
1400: 	/*
1401: 	 * Deleting the current buffer: Need to find another buffer to go to.
1402: 	 * There should be another, otherwise it would have been handled
1403: 	 * above.  However, autocommands may have deleted all buffers.
1404: 	 * First use au_new_curbuf.br_buf, if it is valid.
1405: 	 * Then prefer the buffer we most recently visited.
1406: 	 * Else try to find one that is loaded, after the current buffer,
1407: 	 * then before the current buffer.
1408: 	 * Finally use any buffer.
1409: 	 */
1410: 	buf = NULL;	// selected buffer
1411: 	bp = NULL;	// used when no loaded buffer found
1412: 	if (au_new_curbuf.br_buf != NULL && bufref_valid(&au_new_curbuf))
1413: 	    buf = au_new_curbuf.br_buf;
1414: #ifdef FEAT_JUMPLIST
1415: 	else if (curwin->w_jumplistlen > 0)
1416: 	{
1417: 	    int     jumpidx;
1418: 
1419: 	    jumpidx = curwin->w_jumplistidx - 1;
1420: 	    if (jumpidx < 0)
1421: 		jumpidx = curwin->w_jumplistlen - 1;
1422: 
1423: 	    forward = jumpidx;
1424: 	    while (jumpidx != curwin->w_jumplistidx)
1425: 	    {
1426: 		buf = buflist_findnr(curwin->w_jumplist[jumpidx].fmark.fnum);
1427: 		if (buf != NULL)
1428: 		{
1429: 		    if (buf == curbuf || !buf->b_p_bl)
1430: 			buf = NULL;	// skip current and unlisted bufs
1431: 		    else if (buf->b_ml.ml_mfp == NULL)
1432: 		    {
1433: 			// skip unloaded buf, but may keep it for later
1434: 			if (bp == NULL)
1435: 			    bp = buf;
1436: 			buf = NULL;
1437: 		    }
1438: 		}
1439: 		if (buf != NULL)   // found a valid buffer: stop searching
1440: 		    break;
1441: 		// advance to older entry in jump list
1442: 		if (!jumpidx && curwin->w_jumplistidx == curwin->w_jumplistlen)
1443: 		    break;
1444: 		if (--jumpidx < 0)
1445: 		    jumpidx = curwin->w_jumplistlen - 1;
1446: 		if (jumpidx == forward)		// List exhausted for sure
1447: 		    break;
1448: 	    }
1449: 	}
1450: #endif
1451: 
1452: 	if (buf == NULL)	// No previous buffer, Try 2'nd approach
1453: 	{
1454: 	    forward = TRUE;
1455: 	    buf = curbuf->b_next;
1456: 	    for (;;)
1457: 	    {
1458: 		if (buf == NULL)
1459: 		{
1460: 		    if (!forward)	// tried both directions
1461: 			break;
1462: 		    buf = curbuf->b_prev;
1463: 		    forward = FALSE;
1464: 		    continue;
1465: 		}
1466: 		// in non-help buffer, try to skip help buffers, and vv
1467: 		if (buf->b_help == curbuf->b_help && buf->b_p_bl)
1468: 		{
1469: 		    if (buf->b_ml.ml_mfp != NULL)   // found loaded buffer
1470: 			break;
1471: 		    if (bp == NULL)	// remember unloaded buf for later
1472: 			bp = buf;
1473: 		}
1474: 		if (forward)
1475: 		    buf = buf->b_next;
1476: 		else
